reason: mechanics: sent2 minus sent2_span leaves only 'Fica esperto, ela tá .' — a dangling auxiliary with no predicate; the entire informative content of the sentence is inside the blank, giving no recoverable cue for the idiom.; naturalness: The full idiom 'jogar verde para colher maduro' is a written/proverbial expression, not something Brazilians actually say out loud in casual conversation — spoken usage almost always shortens to just 'jogar verde'. Both example sentences plug the full bookish idiom into otherwise casual speech ('tá', 'pra'), which reads as textbook-stitched rather than authentic.

semantics: cuidado claim is incoherent: '⚠️ No es "ponerse bien" en sentido moral' misattributes a 'moral sense' ambiguity to the Spanish phrase 'ponerse bien', which in Spanish is itself the standard way to say 'to get better/recover (health)' and carries no moral connotation. The real ambiguity (recover health vs. become a good person) belongs to Portuguese 'ficar bom', not to the Spanish equivalent, so the cuidado misrepresents what Spanish 'ponerse bien' means.

semantics: item_full gives 'está bem' as the standard form, but this contradicts the note's own 'ear' field, which says 'tá' is simply the reduction of 'está' (dropping the first syllable). 'tá' phonetically reduces 'está', not the two-word phrase 'está bem'; using 'está bem' as item_full conflates the pragmatic meaning of the reply ('it's fine') with the standard form of the reduced word.

semantics: cuidado wrongly implies Spanish 'fechar' isn't used for 'poner fecha' (dating a document) and that only 'datar' works. In fact Spanish 'fechar' is a standard verb meaning 'to date/put a date on' (e.g. 'fechar una carta'), so the parenthetical '(eso es datar)' misstates Spanish vocabulary even though the core PT/ES semantic gap (cerrar vs fechar-fecha) is real.

naturalness: "Paquete" is not a recognizable Brazilian slang term for menstruation (common real ones are 'chico'/'tio Chico', 'baixo-astral', 'período', 'menstruação'); this reads as a fabricated or non-Brazilian term, and both sentences ('tá de paquete', 'veio o paquete') would not actually be understood/said by a Brazilian, which defeats the deck's core listening-comprehension purpose.; semantics: Unable to verify 'paquete' as genuine Brazilian slang for menstruation; known BR-PT euphemisms are 'chico'/'tio Chico chegou', 'regra', 'está naqueles dias', etc. Not 'paquete'. The item, gloss, and both example sentences all hinge on this unverified/likely-fabricated slang sense, and the vulgar/rare hedge in notes reads as cover for an invented entry — defaulting to fail per uncertainty rule since a wrong vocabulary item here is high-risk.

mechanics: sent2_span 'película do olho' blanks out the disambiguating noun 'olho' itself, leaving 'A ___ fica irritada com o vento' with no cue pointing to a thin film/membrane specifically — could be skin, eyes, face, allergies; not recoverable.; naturalness: Both example sentences use unnatural collocations. Brazilians don't say 'película de plástico' for cling wrap over food — the standard term is 'filme plástico' or 'papel filme'; 'película' is used for phone screen protectors, car window tinting, or photographic film, not kitchen wrap. Likewise 'a película do olho fica irritada com o vento' is a constructed-sounding anatomical phrase nobody actually says; a real speaker would just say 'o olho fica irritado com o vento'.

mechanics: sent2_span 'todavia foi' bundles the connective with the unrelated main verb 'foi' (from 'foi trabalhar' = went to work). Blanking both leaves 'Ele tava cansado, ___ trabalhar', which is ungrammatical/unrecoverable; the span should be just 'todavia', leaving 'Ele tava cansado, ___ foi trabalhar' which is clean and recoverable via the contrastive comma structure.; naturalness: sent2 mixes a reduced spoken verb form ('tava') with 'todavia', which the deck's own notes admit is 'mostly formal/written register' rarely used in casual speech ('people usually just say mas or só que'). No real Brazilian says 'Ele tava cansado, todavia foi trabalhar' out loud — the literary conjunction clashes with the colloquial contraction in the same breath. Sent1 is equally stiff/written-register, undermining a deck whose whole point is spoken naturalness.

mechanics: sent2_span 'largou o namorado' is a longer phrase than the item's surface form: item is the bare verb 'largar' with no parenthetical marking a multi-word target, yet the span pulls in the direct object 'o namorado' too. Removing it leaves 'Ela ___ depois da viagem.' which gives no cue to recover the object, only that something happened after the trip.; semantics: The 'cuidado' claims rioplatense Spanish uses 'largar' in a more limited way than Portuguese. This is doubtful: rioplatense Spanish colloquially uses 'largar' very broadly and similarly to the Portuguese senses given here — 'largar el laburo' (quit the job), 'largar a la novia/al novio' (dump the partner), 'largá eso' (drop/let go of something) are all common. The claimed contrast with Portuguese usage is not verifiable and likely overstated/false.

semantics: The 'notes' and 'cuidado' claim 'pra mim' is informally used 'as subject' where standard grammar requires 'eu' (citing 'pra mim tá bom'), but neither the card's own cited example nor sent2 ('Pra mim, esse plano não vai dar certo') actually illustrates that phenomenon — in both, 'pra mim' is a fronted dative/opinion-marker ('to me' / 'in my opinion'), with 'esse plano' or an implicit 'isso' as the real subject, not 'mim'. The genuine BP colloquialism (using 'mim' instead of 'eu' as subject of an infinitive, e.g. 'pra mim chegar lá vai ser difícil') requires a following infinitive, which is absent from every example given. The cuidado's blanket claim that Spanish 'para mí' 'nunca es sujeto de oración' while Portuguese 'sí puede usarse así' is therefore not demonstrated and is misleading as written, since the opinion-marker sense shown ('Para mí, este plan no va a funcionar') is perfectly normal, identical-structure Spanish too.

naturalness: sent2 'Amanhã vô lá em casa te ajudar' is semantically inconsistent: 'lá em casa' is the fixed deictic expression for the SPEAKER's own home ('at my place'), not the listener's. A Brazilian offering to go help someone at THEIR place would say 'vou aí (na tua casa) te ajudar' or 'passo aí pra te ajudar', not 'lá em casa'. As written, the sentence literally reads 'Tomorrow I'll go to my house to help you,' which doesn't parse the way sent2_en claims ('I'll come by your place').; semantics: sent2/sent2_en mismatch: 'Amanhã vô lá em casa te ajudar' uses the fixed idiom 'lá em casa' which means 'at MY (the speaker's) house', not 'your place'. The literal sense is 'Tomorrow I'll go home to help you', which contradicts the given translation 'Tomorrow I'll come by your place to help you' — the direction of movement is reversed.
